answers:
**1. Where new provider calls should live, and whether the current code follows that pattern.**

Per `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7`, and matching the docstring in `medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py:1-4` ("This module is the single place the chargekit provider client is built. All calls to the payment provider go through the accessors below."), new refund calls belong in `medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py`, as a new accessor function (e.g. a `refund(...)` function alongside the existing `charge()` and `create_customer()`), built on the same module-level `_client`.

**The current code does not follow that pattern today, in two places, and it also doesn't follow it in a third way the doc doesn't even acknowledge:**

- `medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py:4` and `:14` — imports `chargekit` directly and constructs its own `chargekit.Client(api_key_env="CHARGEKIT_API_KEY")` inline, then calls `.charge(...)` on it (lines 14-16), instead of calling `gateway.charge()`.
- `medibill/backend/app/workers/dunning.py:2` and `:9-13` — same pattern: imports `chargekit` directly and builds its own client per loop iteration to call `.charge(...)`, instead of using `gateway.charge()`.
- `medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py` itself is dead code: grepping for any import of `backend.app.billing.gateway` or `from backend.app.billing` across the whole project returns only the doc file and `gateway.py`'s own docstring — nothing in the codebase actually calls `gateway.charge()` or `gateway.create_customer()`. So the "single accessor module" the doc describes is currently unused; all real traffic bypasses it.
- The one file that *does* follow the documented exception correctly is `medibill/backend/app/billing/errors.py:6-9`, which only imports the SDK's error classes (`ChargeDeclined`, `ProviderUnavailable`) and re-exports them — matching the doc's stated exception exactly. (Note: nothing in the codebase currently imports these re-exported classes either, so this exception, while correctly shaped, is also not yet exercised by any caller.)

So today there are two undocumented violations of the "everything goes through gateway.py" rule (`invoices.py`, `dunning.py`), not zero.

**2. `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` vs. actual code — invoice API response and payment integration.**

- **Payment integration** (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7`): claims "Every call to the payment provider goes through app/billing/gateway.py; the only exception is app/billing/errors.py." As shown in Answer 1, this is false as written: `backend/app/api/invoices.py` and `backend/app/workers/dunning.py` both call the `chargekit` SDK directly, and `gateway.py` has no callers at all. The doc names one exception; the code actually has (at minimum) two additional, undocumented exceptions.
- **Invoice API response** (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:11-12`): claims the response "contains exactly the fields: id, tenant_id, amount_cents, currency, status" (5 fields). The actual `InvoiceOut` dataclass in `medibill/backend/app/models/invoice.py:16-22` has 6 fields: `id, tenant_id, amount_cents, currency, status, external_ref`. `external_ref` was added by migration `medibill/backend/migrations/0006_add_invoice_external_ref.sql` and wired into `InvoiceOut`/`charge_invoice` in commit `c23da88` ("MEDB-214 record external payment ref on invoices"), and it is returned by `charge_invoice` (`medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py:17-18`) via `InvoiceOut.from_model(invoice)`. The doc was never updated for this change (`git log -p -- docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` shows only the single initial-commit version).

**3. What should happen to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, and when.**

Per the `architecture-contracts-as-law` skill, doc-vs-code drift is itself a bug, not a "someday" cleanup, and any change to a documented invariant is supposed to update the contract doc in the same commit. That didn't happen for MEDB-197 (dunning worker, direct `chargekit` use) or MEDB-214 (`external_ref` field, `payment_pending` webhook) — both landed without touching `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`. So there is pre-existing debt that predates the refund feature.

Concretely, before starting refund work:
- Decide, for each of the two undocumented direct-SDK call sites, whether it's a real, legitimate exception or should be fixed. Given the skill's preference ("funnel every call through a single module... an enforceable invariant you can literally grep for"), the cleaner path is to refactor `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` to call `gateway.charge()` instead of building their own clients — then the doc's original claim becomes true again rather than needing an ever-growing exception list. If there's a reason they must stay separate, add them explicitly to the doc's exception list instead of silently tolerating them.
- Update the Invoice API section to include `external_ref` (or otherwise reconcile the doc with `InvoiceOut`).
- This should happen now, as its own commit, before/independent of the refund feature — not bundled invisibly into the refund PR, and not deferred, because the doc is already wrong today regardless of refunds. Then, when refund support is actually added, the same PR that adds the new `gateway.refund()` accessor (and any new webhook/field it introduces) must update `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` in that same commit, per the skill's core rule.

**4. The webhook event-type claim.**

`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:16-17` states: "The integration handles 4 webhook event types: payment_succeeded, payment_failed, refund_created, dispute_opened."

The actual handler, `medibill/backend/app/api/webhooks.py:9-20`, handles **5** types: it has the four listed, plus `payment_pending` (lines 14-15), which was added in commit `c23da88` (MEDB-214) without updating the doc. So both the count ("4") and the list are wrong/incomplete as of today — before any refund-related webhook is even added.

This is exactly the failure mode the skill calls out under "Don't cite a number: read the source" — a document asserting a count over something that changes independently of the doc is guaranteed to drift, and it already has. What I'd change: fix the immediate error (4 → 5, add `payment_pending` to the list), and more importantly, stop asserting a count at all — either drop the number and just state the list (letting `webhooks.py` be the actual source of truth for "how many"), or point readers directly at `medibill/backend/app/api/webhooks.py` rather than restating a number that will go stale again the next time a handler is added (as it will need to be, for whatever new refund-related webhook type the provider sends).
report:
## Plan

I set out to (1) understand how `medibill` — the billing backend that already talks to the payment provider — structures its provider integration, so I could say where new refund calls should live; (2) diff `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`'s claims against the actual backend code for the invoice API and payment integration; (3) form a recommendation on what to do about any drift, informed by the `architecture-contracts-as-law` skill; and (4) specifically check the webhook-event-type claim, since the skill has explicit guidance about documents that cite counts.

The repo root contains many unrelated small projects; I first had to confirm which one was in scope, since `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` naming a payment provider only exists in one of them.

## Actions

- Read `.claude/skills/architecture-contracts-as-law/SKILL.md` in full (this is the guide that shaped my approach — in particular its rules that contract-doc drift "is treated as a bug, not as the doc needs updating eventually," that vendor SDK calls should be funneled through one module with a maintained exception list, and "Don't cite a number: read the source").
- Ran `find . -maxdepth 3` to survey the repo; found `medibill/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` and `medibill/README.md` (README confirms `medibill` uses the `chargekit` SDK) — this is the project in scope.
- Read `medibill/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` in full (17 lines) — its three sections: Payment integration, Invoice API, Webhooks.
- Read `medibill/README.md`.
- Listed all tracked files under `medibill/` via `find`.
- Read every backend source file: `backend/app/billing/gateway.py`, `backend/app/billing/errors.py`, `backend/app/api/invoices.py`, `backend/app/api/webhooks.py`, `backend/app/models/invoice.py`, `backend/app/workers/dunning.py`, and `tests/test_webhooks.py`.
- Ran `grep -rn "chargekit" --include="*.py" .` inside `medibill/` — found every file that touches the provider SDK directly.
- Read all six migration files under `backend/migrations/` — `0001_step1.sql` creates the `invoice` table with 5 columns; `0002`–`0005` are no-op placeholders; `0006_add_invoice_external_ref.sql` adds an `external_ref` column.
- Read `medibill/frontend/src/App.jsx` — a stub component, no invoice/payment logic, not relevant to the integration.
- Ran `git log --oneline` and `git show --stat`/full diffs on each commit (`23d2f37` MEDB-180 initial billing backend and architecture doc; `3a7daee` MEDB-197 nightly dunning retry worker; `c23da88` MEDB-214 record external payment ref on invoices) to see when the code diverged from the doc, and confirmed via `git log -p -- docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` that the doc has **never been edited** since it was created in the first commit.
- Grepped for `gateway` and for `billing.errors|billing.gateway` imports across `medibill/` to check whether anything actually calls the "single accessor module."
- Grepped for `billing.errors|ChargeDeclined|ProviderUnavailable` to check whether the one documented exception is itself consumed anywhere.

## Answers

**1. Where new provider calls should live, and whether the current code follows that pattern.**

Per `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7`, and matching the docstring in `medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py:1-4` ("This module is the single place the chargekit provider client is built. All calls to the payment provider go through the accessors below."), new refund calls belong in `medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py`, as a new accessor function (e.g. a `refund(...)` function alongside the existing `charge()` and `create_customer()`), built on the same module-level `_client`.

**The current code does not follow that pattern today, in two places, and it also doesn't follow it in a third way the doc doesn't even acknowledge:**

- `medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py:4` and `:14` — imports `chargekit` directly and constructs its own `chargekit.Client(api_key_env="CHARGEKIT_API_KEY")` inline, then calls `.charge(...)` on it (lines 14-16), instead of calling `gateway.charge()`.
- `medibill/backend/app/workers/dunning.py:2` and `:9-13` — same pattern: imports `chargekit` directly and builds its own client per loop iteration to call `.charge(...)`, instead of using `gateway.charge()`.
- `medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py` itself is dead code: grepping for any import of `backend.app.billing.gateway` or `from backend.app.billing` across the whole project returns only the doc file and `gateway.py`'s own docstring — nothing in the codebase actually calls `gateway.charge()` or `gateway.create_customer()`. So the "single accessor module" the doc describes is currently unused; all real traffic bypasses it.
- The one file that *does* follow the documented exception correctly is `medibill/backend/app/billing/errors.py:6-9`, which only imports the SDK's error classes (`ChargeDeclined`, `ProviderUnavailable`) and re-exports them — matching the doc's stated exception exactly. (Note: nothing in the codebase currently imports these re-exported classes either, so this exception, while correctly shaped, is also not yet exercised by any caller.)

So today there are two undocumented violations of the "everything goes through gateway.py" rule (`invoices.py`, `dunning.py`), not zero.

**2. `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` vs. actual code — invoice API response and payment integration.**

- **Payment integration** (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7`): claims "Every call to the payment provider goes through app/billing/gateway.py; the only exception is app/billing/errors.py." As shown in Answer 1, this is false as written: `backend/app/api/invoices.py` and `backend/app/workers/dunning.py` both call the `chargekit` SDK directly, and `gateway.py` has no callers at all. The doc names one exception; the code actually has (at minimum) two additional, undocumented exceptions.
- **Invoice API response** (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:11-12`): claims the response "contains exactly the fields: id, tenant_id, amount_cents, currency, status" (5 fields). The actual `InvoiceOut` dataclass in `medibill/backend/app/models/invoice.py:16-22` has 6 fields: `id, tenant_id, amount_cents, currency, status, external_ref`. `external_ref` was added by migration `medibill/backend/migrations/0006_add_invoice_external_ref.sql` and wired into `InvoiceOut`/`charge_invoice` in commit `c23da88` ("MEDB-214 record external payment ref on invoices"), and it is returned by `charge_invoice` (`medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py:17-18`) via `InvoiceOut.from_model(invoice)`. The doc was never updated for this change (`git log -p -- docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` shows only the single initial-commit version).

**3. What should happen to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, and when.**

Per the `architecture-contracts-as-law` skill, doc-vs-code drift is itself a bug, not a "someday" cleanup, and any change to a documented invariant is supposed to update the contract doc in the same commit. That didn't happen for MEDB-197 (dunning worker, direct `chargekit` use) or MEDB-214 (`external_ref` field, `payment_pending` webhook) — both landed without touching `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`. So there is pre-existing debt that predates the refund feature.

Concretely, before starting refund work:
- Decide, for each of the two undocumented direct-SDK call sites, whether it's a real, legitimate exception or should be fixed. Given the skill's preference ("funnel every call through a single module... an enforceable invariant you can literally grep for"), the cleaner path is to refactor `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` to call `gateway.charge()` instead of building their own clients — then the doc's original claim becomes true again rather than needing an ever-growing exception list. If there's a reason they must stay separate, add them explicitly to the doc's exception list instead of silently tolerating them.
- Update the Invoice API section to include `external_ref` (or otherwise reconcile the doc with `InvoiceOut`).
- This should happen now, as its own commit, before/independent of the refund feature — not bundled invisibly into the refund PR, and not deferred, because the doc is already wrong today regardless of refunds. Then, when refund support is actually added, the same PR that adds the new `gateway.refund()` accessor (and any new webhook/field it introduces) must update `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` in that same commit, per the skill's core rule.

**4. The webhook event-type claim.**

`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:16-17` states: "The integration handles 4 webhook event types: payment_succeeded, payment_failed, refund_created, dispute_opened."

The actual handler, `medibill/backend/app/api/webhooks.py:9-20`, handles **5** types: it has the four listed, plus `payment_pending` (lines 14-15), which was added in commit `c23da88` (MEDB-214) without updating the doc. So both the count ("4") and the list are wrong/incomplete as of today — before any refund-related webhook is even added.

This is exactly the failure mode the skill calls out under "Don't cite a number: read the source" — a document asserting a count over something that changes independently of the doc is guaranteed to drift, and it already has. What I'd change: fix the immediate error (4 → 5, add `payment_pending` to the list), and more importantly, stop asserting a count at all — either drop the number and just state the list (letting `webhooks.py` be the actual source of truth for "how many"), or point readers directly at `medibill/backend/app/api/webhooks.py` rather than restating a number that will go stale again the next time a handler is added (as it will need to be, for whatever new refund-related webhook type the provider sends).

## Next steps

If I were allowed to make changes, in order:
1. Refactor `medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py` and `medibill/backend/app/workers/dunning.py` to call `gateway.charge()` instead of constructing their own `chargekit.Client`, restoring the truth of the "single module" invariant — or, if there's a reason not to, add both as named exceptions in the doc.
2. Fix `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` now, as its own commit: correct the invoice API field list (add `external_ref`), correct the webhook section (add `payment_pending`, and per the skill, drop the "4" count in favor of listing types without asserting a count or a count derived from source), and correct/update the payment-integration exception list.
3. Add the new `refund()` accessor to `gateway.py`, have the refund API route call it (not a fresh `chargekit.Client`), and update `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`'s payment-integration and webhook sections in that same commit to describe the new call and any new webhook event type (e.g. a `refund_failed` or similar, depending on what `chargekit` actually sends — I have not seen the `chargekit` SDK's source, only how this repo calls it).
4. Consider adding a lightweight grep-based check (as the skill's example script does, and as `clinic-notes/scripts/check_tracked_calls.py` does in a sibling project) so a direct `chargekit` import outside `gateway.py`/`errors.py` fails CI, preventing this drift from recurring silently.

## Confidence and gaps

- High confidence on the code-vs-doc discrepancies themselves: each is directly grounded in file contents and grep results I ran (gateway.py has zero callers; invoices.py and dunning.py import `chargekit` directly; `InvoiceOut` has 6 fields; `webhooks.py` handles 5 event types). Git history (`git log -p -- docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`) confirms the doc has literally never changed since its initial commit, while two later commits changed the things it describes.
- Medium confidence on "what refunds will actually need": I have not seen the `chargekit` SDK's own source (it's an installed dependency, not part of this repo), so I can't confirm what a real refund call or refund-related webhook payload looks like from the SDK itself — only that this codebase's convention is to add a same-shaped accessor function to `gateway.py`.
- Gap: `medibill/tests/test_webhooks.py` is a placeholder (`assert True`) and doesn't actually assert on handled event types or field lists, so nothing in this repo currently enforces the doc's claims automatically — I could not find any CI/test check that would have caught this drift, unlike the `check_tracked_calls.py` guard present in the sibling `clinic-notes` project.
- I did not check whether `chargekit` (or its docs/changelog) has any built-in refund API, since it's an external dependency not present in this working directory as source — I only reasoned from how the repo currently calls it.

1. Refactor `medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py` and `medibill/backend/app/workers/dunning.py` to call `gateway.charge()` instead of constructing their own `chargekit.Client`, restoring the truth of the "single module" invariant — or, if there's a reason not to, add both as named exceptions in the doc.
2. Fix `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` now, as its own commit: correct the invoice API field list (add `external_ref`), correct the webhook section (add `payment_pending`, and per the skill, drop the "4" count in favor of listing types without asserting a count or a count derived from source), and correct/update the payment-integration exception list.
3. Add the new `refund()` accessor to `gateway.py`, have the refund API route call it (not a fresh `chargekit.Client`), and update `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`'s payment-integration and webhook sections in that same commit to describe the new call and any new webhook event type (e.g. a `refund_failed` or similar, depending on what `chargekit` actually sends — I have not seen the `chargekit` SDK's source, only how this repo calls it).
4. Consider adding a lightweight grep-based check (as the skill's example script does, and as `clinic-notes/scripts/check_tracked_calls.py` does in a sibling project) so a direct `chargekit` import outside `gateway.py`/`errors.py` fails CI, preventing this drift from recurring silently.
sections.plan:
I set out to (1) understand how `medibill` — the billing backend that already talks to the payment provider — structures its provider integration, so I could say where new refund calls should live; (2) diff `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`'s claims against the actual backend code for the invoice API and payment integration; (3) form a recommendation on what to do about any drift, informed by the `architecture-contracts-as-law` skill; and (4) specifically check the webhook-event-type claim, since the skill has explicit guidance about documents that cite counts.

The repo root contains many unrelated small projects; I first had to confirm which one was in scope, since `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` naming a payment provider only exists in one of them.
